speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is July 16, 1995. This is an interview with Steve Lewkowicz. The interviewer is Rosanne Krusner. We're in Toronto, Canada. And the language of the interview is an English. Today is July 16, 1995. This is an interview with Steven Lewkowicz. I'm Roseanne Krusner. My name is spelled R-O-S-A-N-N-E K-R-U-S-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in Toronto, Canada. And the language of the interview is in English. Please introduce yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Steve Lewkowicz. And I was born in Poland, a small town, Boleslawiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that is Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't too far from the German border, I would say about three, four kilometers. And the biggest city to mine was Wielun. And the biggest one was Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born December the 2nd, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the time of this interview, how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 70, 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the hometown, what the life is like, the Jewish community, the not Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a small town, mostly farmers, and with a few Jews. I would say approximately maybe about 100 Jews lived in that town. And we had our own synagogue. We had a [POLISH], what you call. And mostly the Jewish people were in business, you know, or were tradesmen, you know, went to villages, brought-- went to where we bought some eggs, chickens, you know, brought in town, you know. And they sold them to the people. And that's how we made a living. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father did. He went to-- to village to village. And he used to buy a couple chickens, a couple dozen eggs, a pound of butter. And he brought them into town. And he sold them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was [? Moshe, ?] Moses, you know. And with the money, you know, we made-- made a living. You know, it wasn't much, but we made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Bluma. And she was a housewife, you know, took care of her kids. I had two little sisters, and me, and my father, and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your sisters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters' name was Gitel and Leiba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about what it was like as a child growing in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I started-- when I grow up, I started to go to school. I went to Polish school in the morning, like from 8:00 till-- till maybe 12:00, 12:30. I went to Polish school. Then I came home. My mother used to make a lunch. You know, it was maybe a slice of bread, a bowl of soup. Then I went to cheder. And I stayed at cheder till about maybe 4 or 5 o'clock. And then I came home. If I had some homework to do, I did some homework. You know. If not the same thing, we had supper, not much of a supper, you know, whatever it was, potatoes, soup, you know, bread. We couldn't afford no meat. That's-- the only meat we had was on a Friday night, on Shabbos. It never failed. You know. It doesn't matter if we had money or not. There was always a piece of fish and-- and a piece of meat, you know. My mother could've made a chicken. There was five of us, and it could of last for three days, the chicken. You know, she got a little tiny piece, you know, but was all this fish. You know. Lots of juice. So we dipped-- dipped the challah, you know, in the juice. And I remember I used to take the-- my mother made a-- baked a cake, made a cake-- used to take it to the bakery. And, you know, used to bake it in, and brought it back, you know, for-- late Friday. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family observe Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other holidays as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah, there was-- didn't miss a holiday. You know, in our hometown, you know there was a Jewish holiday. You know there was a holiday. Because, you know, if somebody made a-- a pot of chicken soup, you know who made a pot-- pot of chicken soup because it's a small town, maybe a hundred people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know each other. And you know. It was always [? chunk, ?] you know, a Shabbos. In the wintertime, mostly it was, you know, you got [? a ?] chunk. I used to take the [? chunk ?] Friday night-- I mean, Friday afternoon-- used to take it to the baker.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the whole week he baked the bread. You know, and it was like a clay stove, big stoves. You know, on Shabbos, it didn't bake. So the stove was still hot, you know, from during the week. So we took it, made a pot, you know, for-- you know, potatoes, barley. And we had a piece of meat, put a piece of meat in, then took in Friday. Then Saturday after shul, you know, I went to the bakery and picked up to pot, you know. You know, she used to wrap it with paper around it, you know, with a rope, you know. That's the-- we had a hot meal for-- for Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you weren't in school, what would you describe as your daily activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly soccer. Whenever we had a chance, you know, we played soccer. Sometimes my little sister used to come down the field. You know. We didn't have no fancy field. We used to play on the meadow. Wherever we found a place, you know, we played soccer. Used to come down. She said, hey, [INAUDIBLE] [? Hirsch, ?] you better come home. You know, dinner is ready. You know. We used to come down. And whenever you had a chance, you know, we used to play soccer. I remember, you know, we didn't have much money. You know. I remember we bought a soccer ball. You know, maybe 10 kids got together. And each one gave a dime or-- or 10 grosze. Grosze it was in Poland, a grosze. We bought a soccer ball. And that's how we played soccer. You know. We never, never played with-- with the Polish kids, always the Jewish separate the Polish because, you know, there were always fights, you know. So Jewish kids would play separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in school, in public school, was this where those fights happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened before you went to school, or on your way to school, you know, or after school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about  an incident as an example that you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most-- most of the time, there's always a fight. They're always, you know, call you a Zyd. Zyd means Jew. You know. The Polish were farmers. They didn't know no better. And mostly, they bothered the kids that had the payot, you know, mostly them. Because they used to pull them by the payot, and they didn't have to fight back. But with me, they didn't-- didn't fight because they know they going to get it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't bother me. You know, you give it to them once, twice, the next time, they don't bother you no more. Then you let them do it, you know, they'll keep on doing it. But they didn't bother me. Mostly, those-- those with the long payot they pull them, you know. And always Zyd, Zyd. But you never-- with the Polish kids, you never went-- they went separate, we went separate. You know, we played-- we played separate. They played separate. They-- we don't mingle, you know, with the-- with the Polish kids. In-- in school, did-- did the teachers treat you differently at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they didn't. They-- they-- the same-- the same way. No. They-- it was not too much of an incidence, you know, not in my hometown. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a member of any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I was, I was in secret. You know. I belonged to the-- I believe it was the-- the Irgun.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Irgun.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Irgun, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or the Betar. You know. And we used to meet on the fields, you know, you see, because it was not allowed to belong to organizations. They used to meet someplace on the field, you know, six, eight, kids. There was one leader. And he told us, you know, told us about, you know, what's what. You know. And but otherwise, nothing. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you have any Zionist tendencies, your family, yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Mostly, you know, you went every day, the same thing. You wake up. You know. It's time to eat. And-- and you went to-- to school, you went to shul. You know, you came home. But the shul, we used-- and mostly on weekends, you know, we went to the shul. You know. But usually during the week we went to the beis medrash. You know. But come Shabbos, you know, you were dressed up. You know there was Shabbos, you know, in the town. You know. And you went to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to synagogue with your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father, my grandfather. And I used to carry my grandfather's tallith, you know, to shul, because you were not allowed to carry your tallith at Shabbat. So I used to carry his tallith. And I couldn't wait till Shabbos was over, like Friday night. I-- I used to get like 10 grosze, you know, a dime, you know, for carrying the tallith. And then Sunday, I went. I bought myself a ice cream or a candy. You know. And I couldn't wait already for it. That's the only time I had, you know, I could get because we couldn't afford. We were lucky we'd afford to buy a loaf of bread. In the winter time, I remember when I went to school, my mother used to make a hot meal. It was a hot meal. We used to go in-- in and buy bread from the baker, stale bread, because it was cheaper. It was two days older, so got it cheaper. And used to take a big bowl, used to heat up a pot of water, and cut the bread up in little squares, you know, and put the bread in, a little drop of salt, you know, put the hot water in, and that was the hot meal. You know, and we was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of student were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, not-- not too good. I was more interested in sports, you know, than in school. You know, but I had to go. You see, like, you know. So-- but I wasn't too bad, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me the townspeople in terms of, was there a very obvious anti-Semitic tone to your town, your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I said-- as I said before, I mean, with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the children, would it affect your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but the same thing. You know. Well, he went to the villages. And there was, you know, they dealt with-- with the Polish people. You know. But that's it, only business. Otherwise, you know, you went separate. We went separate. In other words, no-- no-- no dealing, no friendship with the Polish people. I was a rough kid. You know. I did lots of things in town. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get in trouble a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, plenty of times. Yeah. Plenty of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to go. When I was a kid, you used to steal pigeons. You know, you used to go at night. Pigeons can't fly at night. We used to go at night and get up on top of the rafters, you know, on the balconies. You know. I'd go under the rafters, that's where they-- they-- they stay, you know, sleep. And we used to steal the pigeons, you know, at night. And then the next morning, we sold them to some other people to-- to buy candy or something. And I remember incident in the-- before the holidays when-- like we used to get before the holiday, weeks before, you used to buy chicken or goose. You know, we used to say like you fed the goose. And the chicken used to stuff to make him fat, you know, for the holidays. Because you used to have got lots of Schmaus, you know, you get Schmause. Everybody wanted the fattest chicken, the fattest goose. So they used to have them in the cages. You know. Raised-- you know, they used to feed them good, you know, before the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a couple kids, you know, before the holidays, we used to go out, wake up early in the morning, and let the-- open up the-- the-- the cages, you know, and all the chicken and all the goose, you know, are flying around the whole town. And everybody-- every woman wanted to get the fattest goose and chicken. You know, they were fighting for it, you know, to get the thing. You know. Or, another one is we-- if there was a fire, you know, in town someplace. So what they have? They have a big tank on a wagon. You know. And then horses draw the wagon over the tank.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how they get the fire out. You know, pump out the water. They had to go to the river, fill up the tank, you know, and-- and get the fire out. And so to-- to find out there's a fire, there was a big-- a big bell, you know, in the-- in the city. And they used to ring the bell. Then the fireman that don't know there was a fire, that's how they, you know, know where's a fire. They used to go there and ring the bells, you know, the kids, to make a fire. The fireman there. Everybody is running around the city, you know, to see where the fire was. You know. So there are a of things. You know, as a kid, we did have nothing else to do. So we looked for mischief, you know, whatever you called it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a child in the '30s when Hitler was coming to power in Germany. But were you aware of what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we know there's gonna be a war because the radios. We didn't even have-- not everybody had a radio. And the whole city, it must've been maybe three, four radios. You know, but didn't have no-- no running water, no toilets. Everything was outside. Wasn't-- you know, that way. And the people that had the radio, they used to put it out on the window outside the seal so we could hear the news. You know, so we knew that something is happening, that something's going to war. But I was a kid. I didn't pay attention to it. You know, I just went out and did my own things. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents talk about anything that was going on outside of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. As I said, they were more interested to make a living, you know, to-- to feed us, to make a few cents, you know, to buy bread or piece of meat for Shabbos, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit more about your parents, what kind of people they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, my-- my father, you know, that's-- as a man what he did was made for a living. You know. And my mother didn't-- didn't do, you know, work. You know, just from day to day, that's-- you went, as I said before, you went to village and tried to-- tried to-- tried to-- to make a couple zlotys, you know, to feed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a nickname that you were called by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to call me Schwarze [? Tute. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Tute ?] means a cat, and schwarze means black. Because I was little bit dark, so they called me Schwarze [? Tuta. ?] My father used to call Schwarze [? Moshe, ?] because my father's name was [? Moshe ?] and he was dark to the color. So they called him Schwarze [? Moshe. ?] My mother, they called [? Chana ?] Bluma, [? Chana ?] Bluma because she was a pretty girl. So that's how you know people, you know, by calling-- by-- by a nickname. You know. That's how you did it in the old country, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the nicknames of some of the friends you used to hang out with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of them was limping. They called him, you know, Moshe the [? Calaca ?] you know, or something. One had a hunch back. So they called him, you know, [INAUDIBLE], you know. And one had a-- a split lip. So they called him Yankel with the [INAUDIBLE] on the lip. You know, that's how you know who you're talking about. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change for you at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- when the-- when the war broke out. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the first day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first day, the Germans walked in right at September-- September the 1st, 1939. I was there approximately about 8 o'clock in the morning. Because we are not too far from the border. So the first thing, we went around there. And-- and prior to that, where-- where we used to live, the street was the main street. I could go to the German boarder. So maybe 5 o'clock, maybe 6 o'clock in the morning, we heard by the window we were sleeping, the Polish cavalry, you know, on horses, you know, dressed up in uniforms and with the bayonets or the guns, were going towards the German border. And, you know, so we know something is wrong. But we just get sleeping, and you know, like nothing happened. But maybe a couple hours later or something, we heard them running back. They were running back and the Germans' tank coming in. You know. They were going fighting with the horses. And here the German army came in with tanks and machine guns, you know, and everything. And so I run out of the house. It was right there. I wanted to see a war. I heard fighting, shooting. And because I learned that in school, you know, history, you know, about wars. And I said, oh, here I'm-- I'll see a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time I think I was about 12, you know, something like that. And my mother grabbed me, you know, bullets flying all over the place, grabbed me, and pulled me in. And we stayed maybe a couple hours in the house. And the Germans start coming in. So in back of the house, we had a cellar. You know, we never-- that's how we kept our food if something was left over, you know, the day. We used to wrap it in rags, you know, and the pot, you know, and the rag, you know, and put a stone on top of it, or-- or a brick. And you put it in the cellar, you know, to keep it cool. And put that brick on top of it because of rats running around, you know, they shouldn't get into the soup or the leftovers. So we put a big stone, you know, on top of it. So anyways, that's what we ran into, the cellar. And the cellar was in the back of the house, because we-- the back of the house was a-- like a big square. And the back of the square was the stoves, and like we keep the horses, cows, you know, goats in there. And in the back was the cellar. And that's where we went down there. A family lived right next door to us too. And they had maybe four or five kids. They came down the basement-- the basement. And it was five of us. You know, we went down the basement, in cellar, and we stayed there. You know, because-- And then we could hear the Germans walking upstairs, you know, in the rooms. You know. But somehow they didn't go around in the back. Maybe they didn't know, you know, such a thing as a cellar. We could hear them walking. Because if they would, you know, go around the back and see the-- the cellar, and there's people-- because they'll throw down grenades and start shooting, then they probably would have killed us. Then we stayed there maybe a couple days. And then the houses start burning. When the Germans came in town, they burned down half of the city, you know, start burning. The houses burning next to us. You know, and our house was still standing. They didn't burn it. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at night, we decided that it was to leave the house because we were afraid it will catch on fire, you know, everything. And my father knew-- knew the country pretty good, the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at night, we all walked out, you know, from the cellar. And we crossed fields, you know, meadows. And-- and we wind up on a farm, a farmhouse. And we stayed overnight there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we didn't know where we were. So my father went up on the attic. And there was a little window. And he looked out. And he saw there was a main highway. And the Germans were patrolling the road. You went too far even off the road. And-- and the farmer deserted already the farm. He took off, you know, left everything. And all animals and everything was screaming, howling, you know, because they didn't have-- they didn't fed them. And so he came down. He told us where we were and everything. And we went. We start feeding the animals. We had food for us, you know, whatever they left. And then the Germans found out somehow. They came to check on the farm. And they saw us. You know. They took us out and lined us up, you know, with the machine guns, you know, and took my father away to question. And then they brought him back. And then they said we have to leave, you know, the farm, go back to the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back. In the meantime, my uncle-- I had an uncle living in the same town. He was a tailor-- his house burned down. With-- with my uncle, and his-- my aunt, and two kids, two-- a boy and a girl, they came, and they stayed in our room. And we only had two rooms. This is your uncle on your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother's, my mother's side. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac. And he was a tailor too. And he was burned down. So he came and stayed with us. You know. And we only had one kitchen and one bedroom, you know, we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was what-- four of them and five of us. Nine of us, you know, stayed in the same room. And the kitchen was big enough that we had a bed in the kitchen. So my mother, father, and my aunt and uncle slept in the bedroom. And five kids slept in the same bed in the kitchen. So we used to crisscross, you know, like I slept here, my sister slept here. So she had my feet in their mouths. And I had her feet in-- in my mouth. You know, and that's how we slept. And that's how we stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we worked. You know. And then the Germans made us do the work. We had to clean up the houses, you know, where they burned down. It was bricklaying all of the stones, laying all of it. So what we did is took the bricks from the houses, chopped them down into smaller pieces. And the roads were not paved. You know, it was mud. You know. We didn't have no paved streets. And we used to take the bricks and-- and spread them on the-- on those mud roads so, you know, the wagons can go through or the tanks. You know, the Germans can-- and that's what we did with the-- with the-- with the bricks from the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did there come a time when you were-- did it come a time where it was enforced that you had to wear a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, right away. Yeah. I had to wear a yellow star. I couldn't walk-- we couldn't walk on the sidewalk. You know, we had to walk on the street. We couldn't use the sidewalk. And we'd be set an hour, you know, at night we had to be in the house. We couldn't walk at night. And they made them work. They could-- German could go in the house. And I remember one time and-- and two friends-- she had two brothers. And I don't know what they did or what. Nothing, just walked into-- took them out, took them behind the house, and-- and shot him, killed him. And then my father had to go to the cemetery, dig a grave, you know, and then bury them. I don't know what reason. You know. But-- you know. And-- and-- we had a big square too at the-- in my town. And the-- the floor, the ground was-- was stones, put stones in. And in between the stones grew grass. You know, I remember used to take spoon from the house. And we used to sit on the-- on the ground and dig out the-- the-- the grass between the-- the stones, you know, to keep it clean, you know. Made the rabbi do it, you know, everybody. You know, they made us do the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who delegated the work? Was there a Jewish counsel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. There was like a Jewish leader. And he was in charge of the town. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't. No. I don't

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a ghetto in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no ghetto. We just lived. You know. It was-- it was a ghetto by itself because we couldn't go no place. We all lived in a small town, very small town. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference in the way the non-Jews treated you after the Germans started occupying?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They didn't-- same thing. They couldn't do nothing because they were, you know, under occupation too. You know. So they-- they didn't do too much. You know. And then after I worked in town-- and I don't know how many months-- so I went to work-- to work on a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, my mother said I'd be safer to work on the farm, which the farm was in walking distance, not-- not too far. And I worked there five, six days a week. And the farms-- would take the cows out to the field, you know, and watch them in the pasture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were no fences. Other farmers had land too. And they didn't want the cows to go, you know, on their land. So I had to watch them, they shouldn't cross to somebody else's land. And I used to watch them and from morning, early morning, till late at night. I used to bring home-- and then I used to clean the stables, you know, put fresh straw down, and feed them, you know, and clean them up, and work, whatever I had to do, work on the farm. And sleep, I slept on a-- on a-- hay, a loft, you know, what you would call a loft, where you keep the hay stacked up in the barn. Used to have a ladder, go up on the lofts, sleep there on the-- on the hay. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's farm was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was some farmer's. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish or Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, no. Polish, Polish farmer. And for work on the farm, I used to go home on Sunday, get a couple hours off on Sunday. And he pay me with bread, or milk, or eggs. You know, and I brought it home. And I give it to my parents. And they-- we had enough for us, then some of the eggs they sold, or they bought it, give it to somebody else so we can make an extra couple zlotys and buy something else. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still able to continue his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. He couldn't go on the farm no more. No. No. And-- and I stayed around that farm till a German caught me, found out, you know, that I worked there on the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a few months later. You know. You know. And-- so I had to go back in town. I said, I cannot stay here. I better get back to town. You know, then I continued work in town, you know, cleaning up, you know, sweeping the streets. Like in wintertime, we shoveled snow for one village, the next village because the farmers could go through, you know, the German soldiers, or the army can go from one village to the next one, you know. Because the snow, we didn't have all this equipment here. We used to shovel everything by hand, by shovel.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, was your father and mother perhaps thinking of trying to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You couldn't leave. Where you gonna go? There's no place to-- to leave, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any plans.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No plans. As I say, I was a kid. I didn't know, you know, too much. And so I know that father and mother, you know, whatever they tell you to do, that's what you did. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till they-- till they came in and took me away. The Germans decide to-- first they took out maybe 10 kids-- let's see, between 20 and 30-- you know, to take for labor to-- to someplace else, to take them away from town, you know. So they went. And then maybe a couple months later they said, well, we take now kids, you know, now between 15 and 20, you know, take them. And so I went in the second transport. It was maybe 10 kids of us, took us away, you know. They said you have to be a certain-- a certain time, you know. And this place in the square, you know, you have to be a certain time, and take all your belongings. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here and go to the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
